# Chapter 1 — The Geometry Behind a Fold

*A flat sheet contains every shape it will become.*

Paper folding looks simple. You take a square. You bend it along a line. You press the crease. Done. But every fold is a decision about where energy will go when the sheet bends, and the geometry behind that decision is older than the paper itself.

## Lines, points, and the flat case

Consider a single square. Mark four corners. Mark the center. Connect them with creases that go in alternating directions — mountain, valley, mountain, valley. If you do this correctly, the square collapses flat. If you get it wrong, the paper buckles. The difference is mathematical, not aesthetic.

The principle was formalized by Kawasaki in 1989: at any internal vertex in a flat-foldable crease pattern, the alternating sum of angles around the vertex must equal zero. In plain language: the mountains and valleys around any point have to balance, or the paper refuses to lie flat. This is not a guideline. It is a theorem.

Paper does not know theorems, of course. Paper knows tension. When the angles fail to balance, the sheet stores the imbalance as strain, which it releases by warping. Origami artists feel this before they understand it. The fold "wants" to go a certain way. That is geometry asserting itself.

## Curvature and the limits of flatness

Most folds in everyday objects — a folded letter, a paper plane, a takeout box — assume a flat sheet. But once you allow curved creases, the rules shift. A curve under tension distributes force unevenly along its length. The paper becomes a three-dimensional surface even though every point on it began as part of a two-dimensional plane.

This matters more than it sounds. The hood of a folded car, the petals of a folded flower, the wing of a folded glider — all of these depend on the controlled introduction of curvature. The fold artist is not just making a shape. They are deciding which parts of the paper will stretch, which will compress, and which will refuse to move at all.

Engineers have noticed. Folded structures appear in airbag designs, in heart stents that unfold inside an artery, in solar panels that ship compact and deploy in orbit. The principles are the same as in a paper crane. The materials are the only thing different.

## Why the diagonal is special

A square has two natural axes: the horizontal and the vertical, where the sides run. But the diagonal — the line connecting two opposite corners — is the longest straight path the square contains. Most decisive folds in classical origami exploit the diagonal, because that is where the paper has the most length to give.

Try this. Take any square. Fold it in half along the horizontal axis, then unfold. Now fold it in half along the diagonal, then unfold. Look at the two creases. The diagonal one is longer. It does more work. It also divides the square into two right triangles, which is the starting shape for nearly every traditional model.

This is why the crane begins with a diagonal. This is why the lily begins with a diagonal. This is why the boat, the frog, the swan — all of them — share the same first move. The diagonal is the spine of the form. Everything else hangs from it.

## What comes next

The chapters ahead trace this idea through three layers. We will begin with history, where folding emerges as a craft and a discipline. Then we will examine the crease pattern, the underlying language of any fold. Finally we will return to the diagonal, this time as a working tool. None of this requires you to fold anything yet. But by the end you will see, when you look at a piece of paper, all the shapes that are already inside it.
